Fixture: pathological
Purpose: edge-case source tree exercising filename character classes,
filesystem-state-only attributes (immutable, sparse), and path-length
limits. Backed by mkfixtures.sh because the members cannot survive a
plain git checkout.
File count: 8 (after mkfixtures.sh runs)
Total bytes: ~1,048,648 (mostly sparse.bin's 1 MiB logical size; ~50
                          bytes physical because the body is a hole)
SHA256-of-tree: d902015f4aad3e21f830a4461ad8ac29eea7bb498baea672d9eb973412ad93a3

SHA256-of-tree recipe: see test/fixtures/tiny/MANIFEST.txt; the recipe
is the same and the Go-side helper is shared. The recipe walks logical
file contents, so sparse.bin's hash reflects 1 MiB of zero bytes plus
the trailing "tail\n" suffix.

File inventory (after mkfixtures.sh runs):

  bell\x07char.txt              0 bytes   ASCII BEL (0x07) in filename
  esc\x1bchar.txt               0 bytes   ASCII ESC (0x1b) in filename
  naïve-nfc.txt                 9 bytes   Unicode NFC composed form
  naïve-nfd.txt                 9 bytes   Unicode NFD decomposed form
                                          (paired with naïve-nfc above)
  plain.txt                    14 bytes   ordinary content control file
  immutable-target.txt         17 bytes   test sets chflags uchg before
                                          backup run; cleanup clears it
  deeply/.../file.txt          18 bytes   total path > 200 bytes
  sparse.bin           ~1,048,581 bytes   1 MiB logical, mostly a hole

Edge cases exercised:

  * NFC vs NFD twin: APFS treats them as distinct entries; HFS+ would
    collapse them. Tests detect whether the runner copies both or only
    one (the answer must be: both, because the runner is path-byte
    transparent and does not normalize).
  * Control bytes in filenames: tests that the runner does not
    inadvertently escape, mangle, or refuse these paths during
    manifest serialization or path comparison.
  * Long path (~200 bytes): exercises the PATH_MAX defense in the
    fixture, the path-buffer allocator, and the manifest's path
    column width.
  * Immutable file: validates the runner's behaviour when rsync can
    read but the source file cannot be unlinked in --move mode (test
    will chflags uchg before the run, restore in cleanup).
  * Sparse file: validates that rsync's sparse-handling flag (or its
    absence) is observable in the dest file size.

Materialization:

  Run test/fixtures/pathological/mkfixtures.sh <dest-dir> to populate.
  The script is invoked from test/e2e/helpers.go's SeedSource when the
  fixture name is "pathological". Idempotent; a second run overwrites.

ACs that consume this fixture:
  AC-3   happy-path copy (with hostile filenames)
  AC-5   source mutation detected between T0 and T2 (sparse / immutable
         interactions)
  AC-9   verify intact backup (NFC/NFD twin survival)
  AC-18  path sanitization defeats escape-sequence injection
         (control bytes in filenames must not corrupt the TTY when
         rendered)

Stability:
  - mkfixtures.sh is committed; its output is deterministic.
  - SHA256-of-tree was computed from a freshly-materialized tree on
    macOS APFS; re-run test/fixtures/regen-manifest.sh after any edit
    to mkfixtures.sh and update this line.
  - On non-APFS / non-darwin hosts the script may behave differently
    (Linux ext4 accepts the control-byte names too; HFS+ collapses
    the NFC/NFD twins). The fixture is macOS-APFS-first; tests must
    skip on incompatible hosts via testutil.RequireMacOS.
